revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:旧暦]]
{{Today/Lunisolar/CE/AH|qq=}}
'''Old Calendar''' (旧暦) refers to the calendrical system that was used before a {{ill|Calendar reform|en|Calendar reform|qid=Q1329260|lt=Calendar reform|1=Calendar reform}}. The calendar system after the reform is {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125314718}}. In many countries, since the current calendar is the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}}, it refers to '''the calendrical system before the Gregorian calendar'''.
== East Asia == In many countries of {{ill|East Asia|en|East Asia|qid=Q27231|lt=East Asia|1=East Asia}}, before adopting the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}}, either the {{ill|Chinese Calendar|ja|China暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} or calendars based on it were in use. These calendars are classified as '''{{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|qid=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}''', so the old calendar is sometimes simply called a lunisolar calendar, or—though not strictly correct—referred to as {{ill|Lunar calendar|en|Lunar calendar|qid=Q185688|lt=Lunar Calendar|1=Lunar calendar}} or '''{{ill|Lunar Calendar|ja|陰暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar Calendar|1=Q28692535|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28692535}}'''. In that case, the new calendar is also called {{ill|Solar calendar|en|Solar calendar|qid=Q210953|lt=Solar calendar|1=Solar calendar}} (or yang calendar).
Although there were many calendar reforms prior to the adoption of the Gregorian calendar, compared to that reform they were relatively minor; thus, when the finer details of the calendrical system are not in question, the systems preceding the Gregorian calendar are often collectively referred to as the old calendar.
The old calendars of various countries are essentially very similar, yet their {{ill|Standard time|en|Standard time|qid=Q1777301|lt=Standard Time|1=Standard time}} differs. Consequently, due to time differences the dates of the {{ill|New moon|en|New moon|qid=Q108566|lt=New Moon|1=New moon}} and {{ill|Solar Terms|ja|節気|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Solar Terms|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 二十四節気|1=Solar term|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1190993}} may shift, sometimes causing the same day to differ by one day or even one month. For example, the new moon in the early hours of {{ill|2007|en|2007|qid=Q2024|lt=2007|1=2007}} {{ill|February 18|en|February 18|qid=Q2343|lt=February 18|1=February 18}} ({{ill|Japan Standard Time|en|Japan Standard Time|qid=Q909085|lt=Japan Standard Time|1=Japan Standard Time}}) fell on February 18 in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} and {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}{{citation needed|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to チャイナ}}, but was still on {{ill|February 17|en|February 17|qid=Q2341|lt=February 17|1=February 17}} in {{ill|Vietnam|en|Vietnam|qid=Q881|lt=Vietnam|1=Vietnam}}. Consequently, the {{ill|Lunar New Year|en|Lunar New Year|qid=Q1944136|lt=Lunar New Year|1=Lunar New Year}} ({{ill|January 1 (Lunisolar)|ja|1月1日 (旧暦)|lt=Old Calendar January 1|lt_ja=旧暦1月1日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/January 1 (Lunisolar)|qq=}}) fell on February 18 in Japan and China, and on February 17 in Vietnam. Such discrepancies occur every few years, but in most cases they are resolved by the first day of the following month (in the old calendar), so apart from January the shifting of the Lunar New Year does not cause significant confusion. Note that the standard time used for the old calendar may differ from the country’s official standard time.
=== Japan === In Japan, over roughly the past 1500 years the calendar has transitioned through {{ill|Genka calendar|en|Genka calendar|qid=Q2513041|lt=Genka Calendar|1=Genka calendar}} → {{ill|Gihō calendar|en|Gihō calendar|qid=Q2511433|lt=Gihō Calendar|1=Gihō calendar}} → {{ill|Taien calendar|en|Taien calendar|qid=Q2511463|lt=Daiyan Calendar|1=Taien calendar}} → {{ill|Xuanming calendar|en|Xuanming calendar|qid=Q2511456|lt=Senmei Calendar|1=Xuanming calendar}} → {{ill|Jōkyō calendar|en|Jōkyō calendar|qid=Q2353004|lt=Jōkyō Calendar|1=Jōkyō calendar}} → {{ill|Hōryaku calendar|en|Hōryaku calendar|qid=Q2352988|lt=Hōreki Calendar|1=Hōryaku calendar}} → {{ill|Kansei calendar|en|Kansei calendar|qid=Q2355562|lt=Kansei Calendar|1=Kansei calendar}} → {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|qid=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}} → {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} (the current calendar). Thus, if the calendar immediately preceding the current system is taken as the old calendar, it is the '''{{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|qid=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}'''. The Tenpō Calendar was used for only 29 years (the shortest period of use for any calendar in Japan), yet it continues to be in demand for purposes such as {{ill|Divination|en|Divination|qid=Q1043197|lt=Fortune-telling|1=Divination}} and {{ill|Annual Events|ja|年中行事|lt=Traditional Events|lt_ja=伝統行事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Annual Events|1=Annual event|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11483816}}, and is colloquially referred to as either the old calendar or the lunar calendar. However, as will be explained later, the calendar currently used as the old calendar differs slightly from the pre-reform Tenpō Calendar.
The Tenpō Calendar was adopted on {{ill|Tenpō|en|Tenpō|qid=Q1369045|lt=Tenpō|1=Tenpō}} 15, January 1 (corresponding to February 18, 1844) when it was reformed from the {{ill|Kansei calendar|en|Kansei calendar|qid=Q2355562|lt=Kansei Calendar|1=Kansei calendar}}, and was in use for about 29 years until {{ill|Meiji 5|ja|明治5|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}’s {{ill|December 2 (Lunisolar)|ja|12月2日 (旧暦)|1=December 2 (Lunisolar)|lt=December 2 (Lunisolar)|lt_ja=12月2日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 2 (Lunisolar)|13=User:Immanuelle/December 2 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} (corresponding to {{ill|1872|en|1872|qid=Q7753|lt=1872|1=1872}} {{ill|December 31|en|December 31|qid=Q2912|lt=December 31|1=December 31}}). On the very next day, {{ill|December 3 (Lunisolar)|ja|12月3日 (旧暦)|lt=December 3 (Lunisolar)|lt_ja=12月3日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/December 3 (Lunisolar)|qq=}}, the calendar was changed to January 1 of {{ill|Meiji 6|ja|明治6|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji 6|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|1873|en|1873|qid=Q7757|lt=1873|1=1873}}), and reformed to the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} (solar calendar) via the {{ill|Meiji Calendar Reform|ja|明治の改暦|lt=Calendar Reform|lt_ja=改暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Calendar Reform|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 明治改暦|1=Meiji calendar reform|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125315161}}. The reform was announced on {{ill|November 9 (Lunisolar)|ja|11月9日 (旧暦)|lt=November 9 (Lunisolar)|lt_ja=11月9日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/November 9 (Lunisolar)|qq=}} in Meiji 5 (with {{ill|December 9|en|December 9|qid=Q2304|lt=December 9|1=December 9}} in {{ill|1872|en|1872|qid=Q7753|lt=1872|1=1872}}), and was implemented the following month. It is said that the sudden implementation that year was intended to prevent the government officials’ monthly salaries (instituted after the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}) from being paid 13 times a year (since under the old calendar, Meiji 6 would have included a leap sixth month). For more details on the confusion caused by the reform, see the section on {{ill|Meiji Calendar Reform|ja|明治改暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Calendar Reform|1=Meiji calendar reform|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125315161}}.
However, the transition to the Gregorian calendar was not thoroughly implemented throughout society along with the reform<ref>岡田義朗『暦に見る日本人の知恵』（NHK出版 生活人新書）</ref><ref name="hirayama-1888189">Hirayama Noboru, “Railways Transformed Temple and Shrine Pilgrimage” (Traffic Newspaper, 2012) pp.188–189</ref>. Even the official calendar (the government-issued calendar) in the Meiji 6 edition included dual dating with the old calendar to avoid confusion, and this practice continued in subsequent years<ref name="koyomiwiki-heiki" />. Although this was done in consideration of traditional events and everyday customs, there were recurring debates and criticisms that the dual dating in the official calendar hindered the spread of the Gregorian calendar<ref name="koyomiwiki-heiki" />. During the transitional period when dual dating was being phased out in the official calendar, entries indicating the phases of the moon ({{ill|Moon Phases|ja|月齢|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moon Phases|1=Moon Phases|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11516260}}) began to appear in place of dates<ref name="koyomiwiki-getsurei">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://eco.mtk.nao.ac.jp/koyomi/wiki/B7EEA4CECBFEA4C1B7E7A4B12FB7EECEF0A4ACC0B8A4DEA4ECA4EBA4DEA4C7.html|title=月齢が生まれるまで|work=暦Wiki|publisher={{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|en|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|qid=Q1327713|lt=National Astronomical Observatory of Japan Calendar Calculation Office|1=National Astronomical Observatory of Japan}}天文情報センター暦計算室|accessdate=2014-05-30|qq=}}</ref>. In 1908 (Meiji 41), a proposal concerning the vigorous enforcement of the solar calendar was passed in both the {{ill|Imperial Diet|ja|帝国議会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imperial Diet|1=Imperial Diet|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q704408}} and the {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}}<ref name="koyomiwiki-heiki" />, and from the official calendar of 1910 (Meiji 43) onward, dual dating with the old calendar was discontinued<ref name="koyomiwiki-heiki" />. The discontinuation of dual dating in 1910 was perceived by contemporaries as the “abolition of the old calendar”<ref name="hirayama-1888189" />, marking a turning point in which events based on the old calendar shifted to the new calendar or to a delayed month<ref name="hirayama-1888189" />.
==== Calculation of the "Old Calendar" ==== Until the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo Period|1=Edo period}}, the calculation of the old calendar was based on empirically known constants and cycles—determined using true solar time in {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q740246}}—to establish the timings of the {{ill|Solar term|en|Solar term|qid=Q1190993|lt=Central Terms|1=Solar term}} and the {{ill|New moon|en|New moon|qid=Q108566|lt=New Moon|1=New moon}}. Accordingly, the addition of leap months and the determination each year of the varying month lengths ({{ill|Large and Small Months|ja|月の大小|lt=Large and Small Months|lt_ja=大小月|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small Months|1=short month|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11515765}}, i.e. 30‐day large months and 29‐day small months) were calculated annually.
The time zone used in the current “old calendar” is {{ill|Japan Standard Time|en|Japan Standard Time|qid=Q909085|lt=Japan Standard Time|1=Japan Standard Time}} ({{ill|UTC+09:00|en|UTC+09:00|qid=Q7041|lt=UTC+9|1=UTC+09:00}}), which is roughly equivalent to the [[Solar_time#Mean_solar_time|Mean Solar Time]]{{citation needed||ja|平均太陽時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mean Solar Time|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 太陽時}} at 135° east longitude. In contrast, the {{ill|Longitude|en|Longitude|qid=Q36477|lt=Longitude|1=Longitude}} of Kyoto is 135°46′ east, corresponding to UTC+9:03. Furthermore, due to the {{ill|Equation of time|en|Equation of time|qid=Q186058|lt=Time Equation|1=Equation of time}}, a time difference of up to ±15 minutes may occur. In addition, the positions of celestial bodies are calculated using formulas based on {{ill|Celestial mechanics|en|Celestial mechanics|qid=Q184274|lt=Celestial Mechanics|1=Celestial mechanics}} ({{ill|Astrometry|en|Astrometry|qid=Q181505|lt=Positional Astronomy|1=Astrometry}}). For this reason, the dates calculated using the Tenpō Calendar of the Edo period and those of the current old calendar can differ by about one day or may even result in a change of month name.
Note that since the Meiji calendar reform, the old calendar is no longer official; thus, the {{ill|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|en|National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|qid=Q1327713|lt=National Astronomical Observatory of Japan|1=National Astronomical Observatory of Japan}} provides correspondences between the new and old calendars only for dates prior to the reform<ref>[https://www.nao.ac.jp/faq/a0304.html “What is the Old Calendar?”]</ref>. Meanwhile, even within the same country, the {{ill|Japan Coast Guard|en|Japan Coast Guard|qid=Q565418|lt=Japan Coast Guard|1=Japan Coast Guard}} and its {{ill|Maritime Information Department|ja|海洋情報部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maritime Information Department|1=Hydrographic and Oceanographic Department|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11558993}} have unofficially published correspondence tables between the new and old calendars up to 2010 (with no plans for publication beyond 2010)<ref>[http://www1.kaiho.mlit.go.jp/KOHO/reki/kyuu9700.htm “Correspondence Table of New and Old Calendars, 1997–2010”]</ref>.
However, the National Astronomical Observatory of Japan issues the “Calendar Guidelines” in the first issue of the {{ill|Kanpō (Japanese government gazette)|en|Kanpō (Japanese government gazette)|qid=Q11452096|lt=Official Gazette|1=Kanpō (Japanese government gazette)}} each February—announcing ({{ill|Notification|ja|告示|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Notification|1=kokuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109527857}}) the upcoming year’s {{ill|Solar Terms and Miscellaneous Terms|ja|二十四節気および雑節|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Solar Terms and Miscellaneous Terms|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as well as the phases of the moon ({{ill|New Moon and Full Moon|ja|朔弦望|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Moon and Full Moon|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where {{ill|New moon|en|New moon|qid=Q108566|lt=New Moon|1=New moon}} indicates the first day and {{ill|Full Moon|ja|望|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Full Moon|1=Nozomu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q65240621}} the 15th, etc.). In other words, the official calculation and presentation of 30‐day large months and 29‐day small months is carried out publicly. In 2015 (Heisei 27), the “Heisei 28 (2016) Calendar Guidelines” were published (announced) on pages 25–26 of issue No. 6463, issued on Monday, February 2.
=== China, etc. === In {{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|qid=Q8733|lt=Qing Dynasty|1=Qing dynasty}} (present-day {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=People’s Republic of China|1=China}}, {{ill|Taiwan|ja|台湾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiwan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Taiwan (island)|qid=Q22502}}, and {{ill|Mongolia|en|Mongolia|qid=Q711|lt=Mongolia|1=Mongolia}}) and in {{ill|Joseon|en|Joseon|qid=Q28179|lt=Korean|1=Joseon}} (present-day {{ill|South Korea|en|South Korea|qid=Q884|lt=South Korea|1=South Korea}} and {{ill|North Korea|en|North Korea|qid=Q423|lt=North Korea|1=North Korea}}), the {{ill|Chongzhen calendar|en|Chongzhen calendar|qid=Q846791|lt=Shixian Calendar|1=Chongzhen calendar}}—established by the Qing in {{ill|1644|en|1644|qid=Q6920|lt=1644|1=1644}}—was used. On the Korean Peninsula the calendar was reformed to the Gregorian system in {{ill|1896|en|1896|qid=Q7846|lt=1896|1=1896}}, and in China it was reformed at the establishment of the {{ill|Taiwan|en|Taiwan|qid=Q865|lt=Republic of China|1=Taiwan}} in {{ill|1912|en|1912|qid=Q2077|lt=1912|1=1912}}.
{{Fact|The old calendars in these regions are completely identical. The calculations are based on {{ill|China Standard Time|ja|China標準時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/China Standard Time|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (UTC+8), regardless of local standard times—even in Korea, where the standard time is UTC+9, and in ethnic Chinese ({{ill|Ethnic Chinese|ja|華人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ethnic Chinese|1=Huaren|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q57026211}}) communities around the world.|date=September 2016|qq=}}
In China the old calendar is often called {{ill|Agricultural Calendar|ja|China暦|lt=Agricultural Calendar|lt_ja=農暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agricultural Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in which case the new calendar is known as the public calendar. It is also referred to as the old calendar, the lunar calendar, or as '''{{ill|Xia Calendar|ja|古六暦|lt=Xia Calendar|lt_ja=夏暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xia Calendar|1=gǔliùlì|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4798424}}'''. In Korea it is commonly known as the lunar calendar (or the old calendar). In English it is called “'''Chinese calendar'''”.
In these countries, unlike in Japan, it is customary to celebrate festivals and annual events according to the old calendar that existed before the reform. In particular, the Lunar New Year is celebrated more grandly than the Gregorian New Year. In China it is called {{ill|Chinese New Year|en|Chinese New Year|qid=Q131772|lt=Spring Festival|1=Chinese New Year}}, while in Korea it is known as {{ill|Korean New Year|en|Korean New Year|qid=Q8249787|lt=Seollal|1=Korean New Year}}; moreover, it is a national holiday not only in China, Taiwan, and Korea, but also in countries with large ethnic Chinese populations such as {{ill|Singapore|en|Singapore|qid=Q334|lt=Singapore|1=Singapore}}, {{ill|Malaysia|en|Malaysia|qid=Q833|lt=Malaysia|1=Malaysia}}, {{ill|Indonesia|en|Indonesia|qid=Q252|lt=Indonesia|1=Indonesia}}, and {{ill|Philippines|en|Philippines|qid=Q928|lt=Philippines|1=Philippines}}. In China, besides the Spring Festival, the {{ill|Dragon Boat Festival|ja|端午節|lt=Dragon Boat Festival|lt_ja=端午|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dragon Boat Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 端午|1=Dragon Boat Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1254268}} and the {{ill|Mid-Autumn Festival|en|Mid-Autumn Festival|qid=Q379519|lt=Mid-Autumn Festival|1=Mid-Autumn Festival}} are national holidays based on the old calendar. In Korea, there is {{ill|Chuseok|en|Chuseok|qid=Q1090024|lt=Chuseok|1=Chuseok}} (on {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|lt=August 15 (Lunisolar)|lt_ja=旧暦8月15日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 15 (Lunisolar)|qq=}}) and the {{ill|Buddha's Birthday|en|Buddha's Birthday|qid=Q5348941|lt=Buddha’s Birthday|1=Buddha's Birthday}} (on {{ill|April 8 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月8日 (旧暦)|lt=April 8 (Lunisolar)|lt_ja=旧暦4月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 8 (Lunisolar)|qq=}})). In both Korea and China there is a custom of celebrating one’s {{ill|Birthday|en|Birthday|qid=Q47223|lt=Birthday|1=Birthday}} according to the old calendar, although since some people use the new calendar, simply asking for the date may not clarify which is meant.
{{Fact|In Mongolia, the old calendar in its original sense is the {{ill|Chongzhen calendar|en|Chongzhen calendar|qid=Q846791|lt=Shixian Calendar|1=Chongzhen calendar}}, but it is now hardly used.|date=September 2016|qq=}} Instead, a calendar modified from the {{ill|Time Wheel Calendar|ja|時輪暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Time Wheel Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to チベット暦|1=Tibetan calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q643319}}—which has its origins in India—has been widely used in folk and religious events since before the reform, and it occupies a role similar to the old calendars of other countries. Although the Time Wheel Calendar has never been an official calendar, it is sometimes presented as an old calendar.
=== Vietnam === Vietnam’s old calendar is almost identical to the {{ill|Chinese Calendar|ja|China暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinese Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. However, because it uses {{ill|Vietnam Standard Time|ja|ベトナム標準時|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Vietnam Standard Time|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to UTC+7|1=UTC+07:00|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6940}} ({{ill|UTC+07:00|en|UTC+07:00|qid=Q6940|lt=UTC+7|1=UTC+07:00}}), the dates may differ. The treatment of the old calendar is similar to that in China, and the Lunar New Year ({{ill|Tết|en|Tết|qid=Q912988|lt=Tết|1=Tết}}) is celebrated grandly. Abroad it is also called the '''Vietnamese Calendar'''.
== Western == {{Main|en:Old Style and New Style dates|Dates of the New and Old Calendars|qq=}} In the {{ill|Occident|en|Occident|qid=Q1365619|lt=Western|1=Occident}}, the term old calendar (old style) refers to the {{ill|Julian calendar|en|Julian calendar|qid=Q11184|lt=Julian calendar|1=Julian calendar}} that was in use before the adoption of the Gregorian calendar. Note that in several countries of the {{ill|New World|ja|新大陸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New World|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 新世界|1=New World|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q127834}} the Julian calendar was never used, but the situation is analogous. The transition to the Gregorian calendar took place at different times—from {{ill|1582|en|1582|qid=Q6719|lt=1582|1=1582}} (in {{ill|France|en|France|qid=Q142|lt=France|1=France}}, the Italian states (with some exceptions), {{ill|Spain|en|Spain|qid=Q29|lt=Spain|1=Spain}}, and, under its rule, {{ill|Portugal|en|Portugal|qid=Q45|lt=Portugal|1=Portugal}} and {{ill|Poland|en|Poland|qid=Q36|lt=Poland|1=Poland}}) to {{ill|1924|en|1924|qid=Q2173|lt=1924|1=1924}} (in {{ill|Greece|en|Greece|qid=Q41|lt=Greece|1=Greece}}).
In English, when a date is accompanied by '''Old Style''' or '''O.S.''' it indicates that the date is according to the Julian calendar.
In {{ill|Germany|en|Germany|qid=Q183|lt=Germany|1=Germany}}, because the timing of the reform varied among the {{ill|Territorial state|en|Territorial state|qid=Q1400303|lt=Territories|1=Territorial state}}, it became customary to list both the old and new dates separated by a slash or to append the name of the {{ill|City|en|City|qid=Q515|lt=City|1=City}} to the date. Note that in {{ill|Ancient Rome|en|Ancient Rome|qid=Q1747689|lt=Ancient Rome|1=Ancient Rome}} there existed the {{ill|Roman calendar|en|Roman calendar|qid=Q200966|lt=Roman Calendar|1=Roman calendar}}, a lunar (or solar) calendar used prior to the Julian calendar.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Old Calendar Dates|ja|旧暦の日付の一覧|1=List of days of the Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Old Calendar Dates|13=User:Immanuelle/List of days of the Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} * {{ill|Old Calendar 2033 Problem|ja|旧暦2033rd year 問題|3=旧暦2033年問題|1=2033nian wenti|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Calendar 2033 Problem|13=User:Immanuelle/Year 2033 Problem|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5369027}} * {{ill|Lunar New Year|en|Lunar New Year|qid=Q1944136|lt=Lunar New Year|1=Lunar New Year}} * {{ill|Ancient Calendar|ja|太古暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Japanese Calendar|ja|和暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Calendar|1=Japanese year|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5372133}} * {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125314718}} ({{ill|Solar calendar|en|Solar calendar|qid=Q210953|lt=Solar calendar|1=Solar calendar}}) * {{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}} * {{ill|Japanese imperial year|en|Japanese imperial year|qid=Q1209728|lt=Japanese imperial year|1=Japanese imperial year}} * {{prefix|qq=}} * {{intitle|qq=}}
== References == {{Citation Help|qq=}}
=== Sources === {{Reflist|2|qq=}}
== External Links == * [http://hosi.org When.exe Ruby Edition] – A framework aimed at assigning unique names to, and uniformly handling, calendar dates, calendrical systems, timekeeping methods, calendar eras, and calendar annotations used across diverse cultures and languages throughout history. It supports conversions not only between new and old calendars, but also among ancient calendars. * [https://reki.gozaaru.com/ Western and Japanese Calendar Conversion] – Allows conversion between the Japanese calendar and the Western calendar, down to the day. * [http://koyomi.vis.ne.jp/index.html Koyomi’s Page] – Provides an old calendar and conversions between the new and old calendars (covering the period from {{ill|1000|en|1000|qid=Q1218946|lt=1000|1=1000}} to {{ill|2069|en|2069|qid=Q890591|lt=2069|1=2069}}). * [https://www.paphio.jp/webcal/qreki.html Old Calendar Calculation Library] – A script for calculating the old calendar from the new calendar. * [https://sinocal.sinica.edu.tw/ Conversion Calendar for New and Old Calendars (China Era Names)] – A site that allows conversion between new calendar dates and Chinese old calendar (agricultural calendar) dates.
{{Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|旧暦|version=100066521|comment=Imported full ja history}}
